surviving.prompt_rewrite: Rewrite: enumerate what the framework auto-provides (auto correlation_id/trace via structlog processors + FastAPIInstrumentor span + ObservabilityMiddleware request log/metrics) and instruct NOT to flag its absence. Remove the three template-contradicting flag clauses: (a) missing manual span/metric/log on an auto-instrumented route, (b) missing correlation_id on error paths (auto-injected), (c) missing per-endpoint SLO (template ships fleet-wide SLOs only). Reserve high for a genuine in-domain gap: a mutation/business path with NO structured log of its own, a path that BYPASSES the auto-instrumented seam, or active error suppression. Add scope carve-out: do NOT flag DB transaction/rollback/session state (data-integrity owns it) or idempotency/business correctness (application-logic owns it).

fixture_edits.detail: The one in-domain defect (no structured log on the mutation path) is buried under cross-domain bait (commit-failure, double-prefix idempotency, inline HTTPException import). Strip the bait; pin expect.json to the structured-logging-absence dimension.
Genuinely clean (more instrumented than shipped get_items); should score [] once prompt is fixed.

surviving.notes: Keystone of the over-flag cohort: its three flag clauses each contradict the shipped template. Already has 2 good fixtures, so no additional good fixture needed. Audit-proposed start recall_min 0.50 / fp_max 0.10; re-derive Phase 3.

surviving.prompt_rewrite: Rewrite the trigger: it currently describes the codebase's default-and-only idiom (auto-instrumented engine), so it would flag every query in the repo. Re-scope to instrumentation-BYPASS: a non-instrumented store/path (raw DBAPI call, second engine not registered with SQLAlchemyInstrumentor, background path), a new DB client with no /health, or a data-layer error swallowed without correlation_id. Defer pagination/unbounded-query to performance.

The delete is auto-instrumented like create_item; the 'invisible to observability' docstring is factually false. Replace with new-client-no-health OR db-error-no-correlation-id.

surviving.notes: Coupled prompt+fixture redesign. Both bad fixtures are non-defects -> recall structurally 0. Do NOT set a block_threshold until the redesign produces a true-positive signal (Phase 3). good fixture is fine; no second needed.

surviving.prompt_rewrite: Re-key bullets 1+2 from 'view with no RUM' (the global window error/unhandledrejection handler already covers views) to ACTIVE error suppression: a catch that stops the rejection reaching the global handler / js-errors counter, or an error boundary returning null. Narrow bullet 4 (beacon allowlist) to the unbounded-label-cardinality core; cross-reference privacy for the PII angle (privacy owns beacon PII).

fixture_edits.detail: The global window handler covers the unhandled rejection exactly like App.tsx, so 0/3 'miss' is correct reviewer behavior. Re-author into an ACTIVE-suppression variant (.catch(()=>{}) / error boundary returning null).

INVALID-GOOD: device_class is stripped server-side by the fail-closed allowlist (dead-on-arrival), so reviewers correctly flag it. Add device_class to the allowlist in-patch OR use an allowlisted/server-derived dimension.

surviving.prompt_rewrite: Rewrite to inherit the rubric; replace the unbounded 'at least high' floor with a demonstrability gate (high requires a concrete demonstrable data-loss/atomicity defect). Add codebase-bar note: the session factory sets expire_on_commit=False and created_at uses server_default, so a missing post-commit refresh is NOT a defect (do not hallucinate 'created_at=None'); items.name has no UNIQUE constraint, so absence of de-dup is not a violated invariant. High stays for the genuine planted defect (the in-loop session.commit() that breaks batch atomicity).

fixture_edits.detail: Clean as a data-integrity matter, but its docstring 'no per-row refresh' hands the agent the false created_at=None FP. Align the docstring to the template idiom (note expire_on_commit=False) so it stops baiting the over-flag.

surviving.notes: fp 1.00 on a single good fixture -> borderline-on-precision; add a 2nd good fixture. Expect fp to drop to ~0-0.33 after the codebase-bar clause + good-fixture alignment.

Enforce added/modified-lines-only review (stop reaching into lru_cache/env_file/model_dump out-of-diff). Add scope carve-out: field naming is code-quality/usability, not security.

fixture_edits.detail: GENUINELY CLEAN (api_secret_key: str matches template webhook_signing_secret: str). The over-flag is 100% prompt defect; no change to change.patch needed. OPTIONAL: re-theme to mirror the HMAC idiom for representativeness.

surviving.prompt_rewrite: KEEP the prompt (scope-disciplined, fp 0.00; the reviewers' info classifications for stale-pact / optional-undeclared-read are CORRECT). The recall 0.50 FAIL is a FIXTURE-grading bug, not a prompt or threshold problem.

surviving.notes: Recall 0.50 fix is entirely in the FIXTURE, not the threshold or a 2nd good fixture. fp is a clean 0.00, so no precision concern -> no second good fixture.

surviving.prompt_rewrite: KEEP the prompt's correct high bar (PII to undocumented store / deletion gap missing a store / stale documented-or-indexed derived field). surviving.notes: Recall 0.17 fix is replacing one invalid bad fixture; fp is a clean 0.00 -> no precision concern, no second good fixture. Raise recall_min to 0.90 after the replacement.

Pin the doc-completeness finding at a single severity (low). Add grounding clause (cite the changed line).

The fp 0.33 FAIL is a CALIBRATION artifact, not a prompt defect.

surviving.summary: Realign tests/eval/fixtures/thresholds.yaml usability fp_max from 0.10 -> 1.00 to match block_threshold=None advisory semantics (mirroring dependency/documentation). usability's baseline fp 0.33 'FAIL' is entirely this threshold-semantics mismatch: an advisory agent (engine treats block_threshold=None) scored at a blocking fp_max. This is the ONLY genuine threshold bug in the corpus.

surviving.summary: Do NOT widen any fp_max to 'absorb' a current baseline over-flag — every elevated fp in this baseline is a prompt or fixture defect, not real over-flagging; widening would mask future regressions. After all coupled prompt+fixture edits land, re-derive every numeric recall_min/fp_max via `framework eval-analyze` on a post-fix --repeat sweep (Phase 3). observability-db's threshold is deferred entirely until its coupled prompt+fixture redesign produces a true-positive signal.
surviving.block_threshold: see per-agent entries (high for all blocking agents; null/none for dependency, documentation, usability; deferred for observability-db)

kicked_back.summary: Prepend the finalized shared rubric (.framework/plan21/meta/rubric.final.md) verbatim to every prompt. This is the single highest-leverage change: every good-fixture over-flag in the baseline traces to an agent not inheriting these clauses (severity ladder, codebase-bar incl. auto-instrumentation + hardening-is-info carve-outs, internal-consistency, scope discipline, grounding/diff-awareness, JSON-only output).
kicked_back.prompt_rewrite: Prepend rubric.final.md verbatim above each agent's domain-specific body. Then add the per-agent scope carve-outs and clauses listed in each agent's entry below.
kicked_back._refutation_reason: REFUTE shipping as-is. (1) Blanket blast radius with verification deferred: this prepends a domain-redefining severity ladder to ALL 20 agents at once, including the 9 currently-PASS/healthy ones (privacy, performance, application-logic, contracts, data-lineage, architecture, api-design, accessibility, observability-infra), yet the changelist defers all numeric re-derivation and verification to Phase 3 (threshold-rederivation-001). No per-agent eval gate runs before this lands. (2) Concrete under-flagging risk in a healthy agent: privacy currently scores recall 1.00 / fp 0.00 on a FLAT rule (src/framework_cli/review/agents/privacy.md: 'PII in logs or unnecessary PII collection is high', no ladder, no demonstrability gate). The rubric redefines high to require 'a concrete, demonstrable defect that WILL cause incorrect behavior... a defect that exists today on a changed line, not a speculative future failure, and not the mere absence of an optional hardening' and demotes 'defense-in-depth hardening the codebase does not itself adopt' to 'info at most'. Privacy's whole job is flagging LATENT leak risk; the privacy-001 entry asserts 'outcome unchanged' but that is an assertion, not an eval result, and the new ladder plausibly demotes PII-leak findings below the high block bar. The same risk applies to every currently-clean agent. (3) The dominant codebase-bar carve-out is factually conditional, not absolute: the rubric tells every agent that 'the framework auto-traces via FastAPIInstrumentor / SQLAlchemyInstrumentor... the absence of a manual span/metric/log on a path that runs through the instrumented seam is NOT a defect.' VERIFIED in template: configure_tracing / configure_datastore_instrumentation are BOTH gated on `if not settings.otel_enabled: return`, and settings.py.jinja sets `otel_enabled: bool = False` by DEFAULT. The auto-instrumentation seam therefore does NOT exist at runtime in a default-config project, and a reviewer reading a diff cannot see the deployment config. Instructing the agent to treat the seam as always-present will wave through a genuinely uninstrumented mutation path as 'covered by the auto-seam' whenever OTel is disabled, under-flagging the exact observability defect class Phase 1 is meant to repair. (4) The remaining FAILs that are NOT good-fixture over-flag (contracts recall 0.50, data-lineage recall 0.17, observability-db recall 0.00) are under-finding failures that this rubric does nothing to fix and, via its high-bar ladder plus scope fences (data-integrity owns transaction state, performance owns scans, privacy owns PII), can only make stricter. The corpus correctly routes those to FIXTURE replacements, but bundling them under a single P0 'highest-leverage' rubric prepend that also tightens severity risks net recall loss on already-weak agents. The factual template assertions (expire_on_commit=False, created_at server_default, items.name no UNIQUE, plain str signing secret, no SecretStr/min_length) all verify out, so the rubric is well-grounded for the over-flag cohort — but a change that redefines 'high' for 20 agents (9 of them already passing) and encodes a runtime-conditional seam as an unconditional codebase fact, with verification punted to Phase 3, should not ship as one blanket commit. DEFAULT-refuted stands.

kicked_back.prompt_rewrite: Add a diff-awareness clause: files added in THIS diff are present; only flag a missing alert/dashboard when absent from BOTH diff and tree (an r1 repeat hallucinated a high for an alert the patch ADDS). Pin severities deterministically: PRIMARY wiring break (exporter with no scrape / scrape with no exporter / dev-only obs that never reaches prod / new prod surface with no obs) = HIGH (blocks); SECONDARY parity gap (surface scraped but no alert, or no dashboard) = MEDIUM (stable, no block). Soften the per-alert-receiver clause (template uses a catch-all route).

fixture_edits.detail: NOT-CLEAN: the dev.yml redis block is indented under volumes: not services: (parses as a malformed volume), which r0/r2 correctly flag. Repair the placement under services:.

kicked_back.notes: Repairs cross-repeat severity wobble (high/medium on the same satellite gap) and the diff-awareness hallucination. Good fixture is repaired, not added to.
kicked_back._refutation_reason: Two of the change's claims VERIFY clean (good-fixture repair + diff-awareness), but the severity-pinning clause introduces a concrete recall-regression that the change does not close, so it should not ship as written. Verified-true parts: (1) the good fixture IS genuinely dirty -- dev.yml adds the redis block at 2-space indent UNDER `volumes:` (sibling to pgdata), not under `services:` (confirmed byte-level via cat -A on change.patch lines 9-17), so it parses as a malformed volume; baseline r0/r2 correctly flag this medium, the repair is justified. (2) r1's high IS a genuine diff-awareness hallucination -- it flagged 'redis scrape job has NO alert rule' while the same patch ADDS infra/observability/prometheus/alerts/redis_alerts.yml with a RedisDown alert (patch lines 85-100), so the diff-awareness clause is justified. THE DEFEAT: the prompt_rewrite pins 'SECONDARY parity gap (surface scraped but no alert) = MEDIUM (no block)'. The bad fixture bad/scrape-without-alert is, by name and content, exactly that class -- it adds `job_name: redis` with NO alert/dashboard -- yet it is left UNTOUCHED (fixture_edits only repairs the good fixture). With block_threshold=high, demoting this defect class to medium pushes scrape-without-alert below the block line, so a known-bad fixture (recall 3/3 high today: high+medium each repeat) would stop blocking -> recall regression on the agent's own corpus. Compounding the ambiguity: that fixture's snapshot is batteries:[] with NO redis-exporter service, so the added scrape target ALSO has no backing exporter -- it is simultaneously a PRIMARY wiring break (scrape with no exporter = HIGH per the change's own pinning) AND a SECONDARY parity gap (no alert = MEDIUM), and the change does not disambiguate which the reviewer should follow. Under the new deterministic pinning a reviewer can legitimately rate it MEDIUM and stop blocking. The change must either (a) re-target/clarify bad/scrape-without-alert so it stays a blocking PRIMARY break, or (b) defer the block_threshold per threshold-rederivation-001 before pinning this class to non-blocking. Refuted pending that fix.

kicked_back.prompt_rewrite: Rewrite to inherit the rubric and NARROW the domain to AUDIT-GAP + RETENTION-PATH + ERASURE-PATH only. Defer ALL raw-PII handling to privacy (currently 2 of 3 bad-fixture findings per repeat are privacy's domain). Add codebase-bar clause: do NOT flag structured get_logger().info() of an OPAQUE identifier or absence of a per-line retention policy (the template's own observability middleware and webhook inbox log this way). High is reserved for: a sensitive state-changing op with NO audit record, or persisted personal data with NO retention/deletion path anywhere reachable.

fixture_edits.detail: Valid defect but mis-targeted: its loud defect is raw-email-in-logs (privacy's domain) while the compliance defect (audit gap) is buried. Re-author to a true compliance defect (sensitive-op-with-no-audit-record OR persisted-PII-with-no-deletion-path); move the email-logging case to the privacy corpus.
Genuinely clean (opaque-id structured log). The prompt is what must change to stop flagging it.
Add a 2nd clean good fixture (e.g. a sensitive op that DOES write an audit record + has a deletion path) to widen fp coverage on this fp-1.00 agent.

kicked_back.notes: fp 1.00 on a single good fixture -> borderline-on-precision; add a 2nd good fixture to widen fp coverage. Treat high as ADVISORY (non-blocking) until the rewrite + re-author drop fp from 1.00; re-derive recall_min 0.90 / fp_max 0.10 in Phase 3.
kicked_back._refutation_reason: The change's load-bearing premise is false. It asserts good/audit-logged-action is 'Genuinely clean (opaque-id structured log)' and that 'the prompt is what must change to stop flagging it', but the baseline findings refute this: across ALL 3 repeats reviewers flag a SECOND defect cluster on the good fixture that is unrelated to the targeted PII-logging over-flag, namely (a) list_items(session) called with no limit/offset = unbounded read, and (b) name_filter accepted as input but silently discarded / logged-but-never-forwarded = collected-but-unused personal data. The narrowed audit-gap+retention+erasure compliance prompt will NOT suppress the name_filter finding because logging an unbounded free-text search field (which 'may contain a name/email', per r2 high) with no retention bound falls squarely inside the rewrite's own RETENTION-PATH domain and over-collection/data-minimisation territory the rubric assigns to compliance/privacy. So the good fixture is NOT cleanly clean and would likely STILL over-flag after the rewrite, meaning shipping the prompt change alone does not drive fp toward 0 on this fp-1.00 agent. The entry marks this good fixture action='keep' (no change.patch edit) precisely on the discredited 'genuinely clean' claim. Additionally, re-targeting bad/logs-pii-in-handler by 'moving the email-logging case to the privacy corpus' is asserted without verifying capacity: the privacy bad corpus already owns pii-logged, and the entry provides no concrete new compliance bad fixture (audit-record-absent / persisted-PII-no-deletion), so it converts a currently-detecting bad fixture into a TODO and risks leaving compliance recall untested for its own narrowed high category. Net: the good fixture must be re-authored (drop the unbounded list_items + the unused name_filter) and a concrete in-domain bad fixture authored BEFORE this ships; as written it is not safe to ship. DEFAULT-refuted stands.

kicked_back.prompt_rewrite: Anchor redundant/duplicate-coverage explicitly to LOW/INFO (advisory, never blocks). Reserve high STRICTLY for 'a test that cannot fail' (tautology, mock of the symbol-under-test, interface-mismatch mock, unhappy-path-without-failure-assert). Add codebase-bar note: per-field env-override tests match the existing suite idiom.

fixture_edits.detail: INVALID: test_resolved_log_level_prod_is_info is a VERBATIM duplicate of an existing parametrized [prod] case (visible via the bundle context), which reviewers correctly flag. Replace the duplicate with a genuinely additive distinct-behavior test.
Add a 2nd clean good fixture (a meaningful additive test) to widen fp coverage on this fp-1.00 agent.

kicked_back.notes: fp 1.00 driven by the verbatim-duplicate test + an unanchored prompt that rated the SAME duplicate high/low/medium across repeats. Coupled fixture+prompt fix; add a 2nd good fixture to widen coverage. Recall (a-test-that-cannot-fail) is perfect and stable.
kicked_back._refutation_reason: The fixture_edits leave the good fixture dirty after shipping, so this fp-1.00 agent does not cleanly recover. good/meaningful-assert/change.patch adds TWO tests, and the baseline flagged BOTH: (1) test_resolved_log_level_prod_is_info (verified verbatim duplicate of the template's parametrized test_resolved_log_level_is_info_outside_dev over ['staging','prod','ci'] at template/tests/unit/test_settings.py.jinja:18-20), and (2) test_slo_request_latency_p99_env_override, which reviewers flagged as redundant low-severity in r0 and r2 because the template's test_env_vars_override (line 27-32) already proves a float field (APP_SLO_ERROR_RATE_PCT) is env-overridden and pydantic-settings coerces every scalar identically. The change's replace-redundant-test edit ONLY removes the log-level duplicate; it never touches the SLO env-override test. After the fix lands, the good fixture STILL draws a redundant-coverage finding on the SLO test. Combined with the prompt rewrite that re-anchors redundant/duplicate coverage to low/info, that finding no longer blocks at block_threshold=high, so the fixture trivially 'passes' the gate WITHOUT being clean -- it will not yield a literal []. The note claims the coupled fix drives fp from 1.00 down, but it cannot reach 0 on the primary good fixture because a real redundant-coverage over-flag remains by construction; the precision win is masked by the threshold semantics rather than earned by a genuinely-clean fixture. Recommend tightening the fixture_edit to also collapse test_slo_request_latency_p99_env_override into the existing env-override idiom before shipping. DEFAULT-refute uncertainty on the unaddressed second over-flag stands.

kicked_back.prompt_rewrite: Prepend rubric (replace the ad-hoc flat rule; outcome unchanged — PII logged/echoed/captured-into-telemetry stays high). Add an explicit scope line: personal-data only; defer authz->security, validation/correctness->application-logic, contract->contracts, observability coverage->observability. privacy is the SOLE owner of whether personal data leaks; other agents cross-reference but never re-flag.

Add a 2nd good fixture (pii-redacted-log: an opaque-id structured log that is correctly clean) to widen fp coverage; only 1 good case exists today on this high-stakes sole-owner agent.

kicked_back.notes: Cleanest agent in the corpus (recall 1.00 / fp 0.00) but only ONE good fixture. Borderline-on-precision-COVERAGE rather than borderline-on-fp: a single good case gives weak fp signal for the agent that owns every PII boundary, so add a 2nd good fixture (synthesis-recommended). recall_min 0.90 / fp_max 0.10.
kicked_back._refutation_reason: The bad-fixture edit (de-compound pii-in-response 'to a single response-leak so the expected dimension is unambiguous') is unjustified and risks regressing a healthy, sole-owner, high-stakes agent (privacy is the cleanest in the corpus: recall 1.00 / fp 0.00). (1) The stated rationale is false against the actual grading mechanism: detection in evals.py flags() = ANY finding at/above block_threshold in the seeded file, and expect.json grades only {"file": "src/demo/routes/items.py"} (no per-dimension grading). A compound bad fixture therefore causes NO 'expected dimension' ambiguity -- the agent already passes by flagging either dimension, so de-compounding fixes a non-problem. (2) The bad fixture plants TWO distinct, real defect classes that reviewers caught reliably across all 3 baseline repeats: response-leak (owner_email added to ItemRead/response_model -> echoed on GET+POST; high in r0/r1/r2) AND unnecessary-collection (ItemCreate accepts owner_email but post_item calls create_item(session, payload.name), silently dropping it; high in r0/r1, medium in r2). Stripping the collection axis removes a reliably-detected high-severity defect class (accept-then-drop PII / over-collection), shrinking recall coverage on the agent that owns every PII boundary. (3) It breaks a deliberate good/bad symmetry: good/pii-excluded is clean precisely because it PERSISTS the collected email (create_item(session, payload.name, owner_email=payload.owner_email)) AND EXCLUDES it from ItemRead -- the exact inverse of the bad fixture's leak-and-drop. De-compounding the bad case to response-leak-only destroys the matched discriminator that proves the agent correctly clears justified-collection. The prompt rewrite (prepend rubric, scope line) is benign and outcome-neutral and the additive 2nd good fixture is fine, but the change as specified ships an unjustified fixture edit that should not ship. DEFAULT-refuted holds.

kicked_back.prompt_rewrite: Prepend rubric; add an explicit CORRECTNESS-only domain line and a high/medium calibration line (a defect real TODAY = high; a latent fragility relying on accidental correctness = medium). Add codebase-bar note: the template uses both `if not X` and `if X is None` legitimately — do not flag the codebase's own falsy guards. Scope carve-out: defer contract/schema/api-design/security/data-integrity concerns.

fixture_edits.detail: WEAK/BORDERLINE + factually-WRONG inline comment ('returns 404 for any id=0' is false — item is the ORM object, not item_id). Fix the comment to state the real latent-ORM-truthiness defect; optionally upgrade to an unambiguous present-tense defect.

kicked_back.notes: PASS today (recall 1.00 / fp 0.00). No precision concern -> no second good fixture. Treat medium as advisory until a true-high bad fixture exists.
kicked_back._refutation_reason: REFUTED on a concrete blocking-recall regression. Verified registry.py line 91-104: application-logic's PRODUCTION block_threshold is 'info' (positional arg 3), and AgentSpec (line 41) confirms info<medium<high. The change sets block_threshold='high' and openly concedes 'Treat medium as advisory until a true-high bad fixture exists.' But the agent's ONLY bad fixture (tests/eval/fixtures/application-logic/bad/falsy-none-check) is the latent ORM-truthiness defect ('if not item' vs 'if item is None' on session.get), and all three baseline runs (findings/application-logic/bad/falsy-none-check__r0-r2.json) detect it UNANIMOUSLY at severity 'medium' (recall 1.00, fp 0.00; thresholds.yaml recall_min 0.90). Under today's info threshold that medium finding BLOCKS; under the proposed high threshold it no longer blocks. There is no high-severity bad fixture in the corpus (only falsy-none-check exists, expect.json carries no severity), so the change drops the agent's effective BLOCKING recall against its entire tested defect class to ZERO while leaving non-blocking recall cosmetically at 1.00 — a real correctness defect class (accidental-correctness/falsy-None sentinel) silently stops gating merges. The falsy/None bug is a genuine latent defect (the reviewers rate it medium correctly), so demoting it below the block bar is under-flagging a real class, not pruning a false positive. The prompt rewrite + fixture comment-fix are reasonable in isolation, but they ride the same change object as the block_threshold raise, so the change as written ships a recall-destroying threshold loosening with no high-severity fixture to justify it. Refute shipping until block_threshold stays at info/medium (or a true high bad fixture is added first).

kicked_back.prompt_rewrite: Prepend rubric; allow stepping a single non-essential-control finding down to medium. Otherwise unchanged.

fixture_edits.detail: Add a good+bad pair for the div-onClick-as-button category (untested high category).
Add a good+bad pair for keyboard-inaccessibility (untested high category). Only img-alt is currently exercised.

kicked_back.notes: Healthy (bad_recall 1.00 / good_fp 0.00). The added good fixtures are coverage for two untested high categories, not a precision rescue -> needs_second_good_fixture=false (coverage-driven add, tracked under fixture_edits).
kicked_back._refutation_reason: The prompt rewrite introduces an under-specified severity-loosening clause -- 'allow stepping a single non-essential-control finding down to medium' -- with no definition of what makes a control 'non-essential'. Combined with the unchanged block_threshold=high (a medium does NOT block), this creates a concrete path for a genuine accessibility high (missing alt / div-onClick-as-button / keyboard-inaccessibility) to be silently downgraded to a non-gating medium, i.e. real under-flagging of a WCAG defect class. No fixture exercises this downgrade boundary, so the loosened severity is untested. Compounding the risk: the change ADDS two unproven good+bad pairs (div-onClick-as-button, keyboard-inaccessibility) for the two high categories most likely to be hit by the downgrade clause, yet has zero baseline grading evidence for them -- the only verified data is the single img-no-alt path (bad recall 1.00 high 3/3, good accessible-img fp 0.00 / [] 3/3). The agent is already HEALTHY (recall 1.00 / fp 0.00) with no false-positive problem to solve, so the downgrade clause buys nothing on precision while opening an under-flagging seam. Per the default-to-refuted directive given a concrete, file-verified objection, refute shipping as-is: either drop the 'step down to medium' clause (keep 'otherwise unchanged') or define 'non-essential-control' tightly and add a fixture that pins the medium severity before merging.
